name: scaffolding notes
creation_notes: How guided notes reduce cognitive load while promoting independence.

seo_structure.title_tag_suggestion: Scaffolding Notes: Strategies for Every Classroom (2025)
seo_structure.meta_description_suggestion: Learn how scaffolding notes reduce cognitive load and support diverse learners. Practical strategies, templates, and differentiation tips for K-12 teachers.

qapeds.gate1_reason: Title is declarative ('How Scaffolding Notes Transform Learning for Diverse Classrooms'), not interrogative. No explicit interrogative question appears within the first 120 words of body text. The first question appears deep in the article under an H2.
qapeds.gate2_reason: No structurally isolatable canonical answer (1-3 sentences) appears within the first 150 words. The intro weaves a scenario narrative and mentions scaffolding notes in passing. The actual definition appears under a later H2 section.

strengths: Well-structured differentiation table mapping student need levels to scaffolding types, with clear descriptions — this is the most citable element in the article
Logical content progression from definition through cognitive load rationale to implementation to independence, following a natural pedagogical arc
FAQ section with five relevant questions provides strong schema potential and addresses real teacher concerns about scaffolding notes

recommendations.issue: Zero named sources or attributed research — 'research from cognitive scientists shows' and 'research shows' appear without any specific citation, violating the 'NEVER use research shows without a named source' rule
recommendations.fix: Add at minimum 3 named citations: (1) Sweller's Cognitive Load Theory (1988/2011) for the cognitive load claims, (2) Fisher & Frey's Gradual Release of Responsibility framework (2013) for the independence progression, (3) a specific study on guided notes efficacy such as Haydon et al. (2011) or Austin et al. (2002). Include year, author, and finding for each.

recommendations.issue: No extractable direct-answer block in the first 150 words — the intro is a narrative scenario that delays the definition and core answer
recommendations.fix: Add a standalone 40-60 word answer block immediately after the opening scenario (before the Wayground mention): 'Scaffolding notes are structured, partially completed templates that reduce cognitive load by providing prompts, sentence frames, and visual cues. They guide students toward core concepts while freeing working memory for deeper understanding, making note-taking accessible across reading levels and language backgrounds.'

recommendations.issue: Statistical density is near zero — no percentages, no study sample sizes, no effect sizes, no data points anywhere in the article
recommendations.fix: Add 3-4 specific statistics with attribution. Examples: cite retention improvement percentages from guided notes research, cognitive load reduction metrics, or engagement data. If using Wayground platform data, use 'Based on X Wayground educators' framing.

recommendations.issue: URL slug is missing entirely — article cannot be published without one, and the missing slug scores 0
recommendations.fix: Set slug to 'scaffolding-notes' — matches primary keyword exactly, is concise (18 chars), lowercase with hyphens.

recommendations.issue: No first-hand practitioner experience signals — Maria, James, and Aisha are clearly fictional illustrative characters, not real classroom data or teacher testimony
recommendations.fix: Replace or supplement fictional scenarios with real practitioner quotes or Wayground-sourced teacher feedback. Use patterns like 'A 7th-grade science teacher in Houston found that...' or 'Based on feedback from Wayground's teacher vetting network...' to signal genuine experience.

brand_issues.fix: Convert all headings to sentence case: capitalize only the first word and proper nouns/product names. Example: 'How scaffolding notes transform learning for diverse classrooms' (H1); 'What are scaffolding notes? Benefits backed by learning science' (H2); 'The foundation: structured pages that free up thinking space' (H2); 'How cognitive load research supports every learner' (H2); 'Streamlined differentiation in one lesson' (H2); 'Guided notes that reduce cognitive load and boost participation' (H2); 'Implementing scaffolding notes to foster independence' (H2); 'Scaffolding notes in diverse classrooms: frequently asked questions' (H2); 'Bring scaffolding notes to life with flexible, teacher-first tools' (H2)

instances: 'What Are Scaffolding Notes? Benefits Backed by Learning Science' (H2 — opens with interrogative)
'How do scaffolding notes support students with different learning needs?' (FAQ H3)
'What's the difference between scaffolding notes and regular guided notes?' (FAQ H3)
'How can I create scaffolding notes without spending hours on prep?' (FAQ H3)
'Do scaffolding notes work for students who are advanced readers?' (FAQ H3)
'How do I know when to reduce the scaffolding in my notes?' (FAQ H3)
brand_issues.fix: Rewrite the non-FAQ H2 as a declarative statement: e.g., 'What scaffolding notes are and why they work' or 'Defining scaffolding notes: the learning science behind them'. For FAQ subheadings: the brand rule prohibits question-form headings universally. Consider converting FAQ H3s to declarative statements followed by the answer, or use a dedicated FAQ schema block with non-heading question labels (e.g., bold text rather than H3 tags). Example rewrites: 'How scaffolding notes support different learning needs' | 'Scaffolding notes vs. regular guided notes' | 'Creating scaffolding notes without extra prep' | 'Scaffolding notes for advanced readers' | 'Knowing when to reduce scaffolding'.

instances: 'built-in accommodations that save prep time while reaching every student'
brand_issues.fix: Follow 'save prep time' with 'time for what?' context. Example: '...that save prep time so you can spend more time on relationships and instruction.'

instances: 'Research from cognitive scientists shows that when we guide students' attention to the right places, comprehension and retention improve across all readiness levels.'
'Research shows that structured note formats improve both motivation and retention.'
brand_issues.fix: Name the source. Replace 'Research from cognitive scientists shows' with 'According to [Named Researcher/Institution, Year]...' Per brand standards, 'research shows' without a named source is a weak signal and reduces trustworthiness. Two unnamed citations flagged.

brand_positive_signals: Strong concrete classroom scenario (Maria, James, Aisha) opens the article — practical and teacher-facing
Correct use of 'Wayground' throughout; zero 'quiz tool/app' language
Product mention count is exactly 3 — at the maximum allowed without penalty
No em dashes found in article body
No 'ELL,' 'SPED,' 'modifications,' or other forbidden vocabulary found
Technology positioned as amplifying teacher expertise — 'Technology works best when it amplifies your expertise and keeps the human connection at the center of learning' is well-voiced
Gradual release / fading framework is explained practically, not theoretically
Benefits framed before features in product mentions
geo_narrative:
## GEO Evaluation: Scaffolding Notes

### Executive Summary

This article scores a **partial GEO composite of 35.95** (without brand voice), placing it well below the publish-ready threshold. The fundamental weaknesses are **zero named sources** (the most damaging single gap), **no extractable answer block** in the opening, and **no statistical evidence** anywhere in the body. The article has structural potential — a useful differentiation table and a well-formed FAQ section — but these assets are undermined by an almost complete absence of authority signals, which cripples performance across every AI citation platform.

---

### GEO Composite Score Breakdown

| Dimension | Score | Weight | Weighted Contribution |
|-----------|-------|--------|-----------------------|
| AI Citability | 41 | 25% | 10.25 |
| Content E-E-A-T | 33 | 20% | 6.60 |
| Platform Optimization | 33 | 20% | 6.60 |
| Brand Voice | *null* | 15% | — *(orchestrator)* |
| SEO Structure | 50 | 10% | 5.00 |
| Schema Recommendations | 75 | 10% | 7.50 |
| **Partial Total (85%)** | — | — | **35.95** |

---

### QAPEDS Score Breakdown

| Dimension | Score | Rationale |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| **Q** (Question) | 4 / 10 | ⚠️ GATE 1 FAIL — Title is declarative, not interrogative. No explicit question in first 120 words. Capped at ≤5. |
| **A** (Answer) | 4 / 10 | ⚠️ GATE 2 FAIL — Definition is buried under an H2 deep in the article. Intro is narrative scenario, not a standalone answer. Capped at ≤5. |
| **P** (Proof) | 2 / 10 | Two instances of "research shows" with zero attribution. No named researchers, no studies, no statistics, no expert quotes. |
| **E** (Expansion) | 7 / 10 | Strong logical progression: definition → theory → differentiation → implementation → independence → FAQ. Minor redundancy between intro and first H2. |
| **D** (Decision) | 6 / 10 | Three-tier table is excellent for decision-making. Choice-based implementation advice is practical. Lacks trade-offs or limitations of scaffolding notes. |
| **S** (Structure) | 4 / 10 | Answer isolation capped at ≤1 due to Gate 2 failure. Headers, bullets, and table are good. FAQ section is well-formed. |
| **QAPEDS Composite** | **4.25** | **Verdict: Weak (<6.0)** |

---

### Gate Failures

**Gate 1 — Explicit Question Gate: FAILED**
The title "How Scaffolding Notes Transform Learning for Diverse Classrooms" is a declarative statement, not an interrogative question. The first 120 words of body text contain a narrative classroom scenario but no explicit question like "What are scaffolding notes?" or "How do scaffolding notes reduce cognitive load?" The first question in the article ("Looking for ways to make note-taking accessible for everyone?") is a rhetorical marketing question, not the primary query anchor.

**Gate 2 — Direct Answer Gate: FAILED**
The first 150 words describe Maria, James, and Aisha's struggles, then vaguely reference "structured, partially completed note templates" embedded in narrative. The canonical definition ("They're structured, partially completed note pages that guide students toward the most important ideas while freeing up brainpower for actual understanding") doesn't appear until the second H2 section, well past the 150-word mark. No isolatable answer block exists in the opening.

---

### Per-Dimension Deep Dive

#### AI Citability: 41/100
The article's most critical deficit. Without a direct answer block in the opening, AI systems have nothing to extract for snippet responses. The differentiation table (High/Moderate/Low support) is the single strongest citable element — it's self-contained, structured, and directly useful. However, zero statistical density (no numbers, percentages, or attributed data anywhere) makes the article invisible to citation algorithms that weight factual density. The GEO research paper found statistics improve citability by +33%; this article has none.

#### Content E-E-A-T: 33/100
This is the weakest dimension and the most urgent fix. Authoritativeness scores 15/100 — the lowest possible range for an article that at least discusses a real pedagogical concept. "Research from cognitive scientists shows" without naming Sweller, Paas, or any specific study is an anti-pattern explicitly flagged in the brand voice guide ("NEVER: 'research shows' without a named source"). The fictional classroom scenarios (Maria, James, Aisha) read as AI-generated illustrations, not real practitioner experience. No teacher quotes, no Wayground user data, no institutional references.

#### Platform Optimization: 33/100
No platform flags pass. Google AIO requires a direct answer block + named citation — this article has neither. ChatGPT needs factual density to select content for responses — this article offers generalizations. Perplexity requires ≥2 named stats per 500 words — this article has zero across ~1,200 words. The FAQ section gives Perplexity potential, but without factual anchors, the answers read as opinion rather than sourced knowledge.

#### SEO Structure: 50/100
The missing URL slug is a zero-score component that drags down the entire dimension. The SEO title (57 chars) is within range but uses "Transform" — a vague, overused verb. Meta description is adequate length but ends with a promotional CTA ("try Wayground's tools today") that wastes valuable description space. Keyword placement is solid: "scaffolding notes" appears in H1, early body text, multiple H2s, and the conclusion.

#### Schema Recommendations: 75/100
The article clearly supports Article schema (always), FAQPage schema (five well-formed Q&A pairs), and LearningResource schema (educational content targeting teachers). The FAQ section is the article's strongest structural asset for schema implementation. HowTo schema is borderline — the implementation section has sequential steps but isn't formatted as an explicit step-by-step guide.

---

### Top 5 Prioritized Recommendations

1. **🔴 [Authority] Add named citations — E-E-A-T: Authoritativeness**
   Replace both instances of "research shows/from cognitive scientists" with specific attributed citations. At minimum: Sweller's Cognitive Load Theory (1988), Fisher & Frey's Gradual Release of Responsibility (2013), and one empirical guided-notes study (e.g., Haydon et al., 2011). Include author, year, and specific finding for each.

2. **🔴 [Structural] Add a direct-answer block in the first 150 words — AI Citability**
   Insert a standalone 40–60 word definition paragraph immediately after the opening scenario: "Scaffolding notes are structured, partially completed templates that reduce cognitive load by providing prompts, sentence frames, and visual cues. They guide students toward core concepts while freeing working memory for deeper understanding, making note-taking accessible across reading levels and language backgrounds."

3. **🔴 [Content] Add statistical evidence — AI Citability: Statistical Density**
   Include 3–4 specific data points with attribution throughout the article. Examples: retention improvement percentages from guided notes research, cognitive load reduction metrics, or engagement data from Wayground's platform ("Based on 200+ Wayground educators...").

4. **🟡 [Formatting] Set URL slug — SEO Structure**
   Assign slug: `scaffolding-notes`. Matches primary keyword exactly, 18 characters, lowercase with hyphen.

5. **🟡 [Content] Replace fictional scenarios with real practitioner evidence — E-E-A-T: Experience**
   Supplement or replace Maria/James/Aisha with real teacher quotes, Wayground user feedback, or attributed classroom examples ("A 7th-grade teacher in Houston found that..." or "Based on feedback from Wayground's teacher network...").

---

### Pre-Publish Checklist

| Item | Recommendation |
|------|----------------|
| **Schema to add** | `Article`, `FAQPage`, `LearningResource` |
| **Title tag** | `Scaffolding Notes: Strategies for Every Classroom (2025)` (54 chars) |
| **Meta description** | `Learn how scaffolding notes reduce cognitive load and support diverse learners. Practical strategies, templates, and differentiation tips for K-12 teachers.` (156 chars) |
| **URL slug** | `scaffolding-notes` |

---

**Bottom line:** This article has a solid pedagogical framework and good structural bones (table, FAQ, logical progression), but it is **not ready for publication** in its current form. The complete absence of named sources and statistical evidence makes it functionally invisible to AI citation engines and violates the brand guide's explicit rules on attribution. Fixing the top three recommendations (named citations, answer block, statistics) would likely lift the GEO composite by 20–25 points.
brand_narrative:
I'll systematically audit this article against every brand rule before producing the output.

**Scratchpad audit:**

**HARD FAIL checks:**

A. Naming: "Wayground" used correctly. No "Quizizz" found. No "quiz tool/app/maker/platform" found. PASS.

B. Em dashes: Scanning... I see "—" in the article body? Let me check carefully. The article uses commas, colons, and hyphens. I don't see any `—` em dashes in the body text. PASS.

C. Heading case: 
- "How Scaffolding Notes Transform Learning for Diverse Classrooms" — H1, sentence case? "How" capitalized (first word), "Scaffolding," "Notes," "Transform," "Learning," "Diverse," "Classrooms" — this is TITLE CASE. HARD FAIL.
- "What Are Scaffolding Notes? Benefits Backed by Learning Science" — Title case ("What Are Scaffolding Notes? Benefits Backed by Learning Science") — HARD FAIL on title case.
- "The Foundation: Structured Pages That Free Up Thinking Space" — Title case. HARD FAIL.
- "How Cognitive Load Research Supports Every Learner" — Title case. HARD FAIL.
- "Streamlined Differentiation in One Lesson" — Title case. HARD FAIL.
- "Guided Notes That Reduce Cognitive Load and Boost Participation" — Title case. HARD FAIL.
- "Implementing Scaffolding Notes To Foster Independence" — Title case. HARD FAIL.
- "Scaffolding Notes In Diverse Classrooms: Frequently Asked Questions" — Title case. HARD FAIL.
- "Bring Scaffolding Notes to Life With Flexible, Teacher-First Tools" — Title case. HARD FAIL.

Wait, let me reconsider. Sentence case means only the first word and proper nouns are capitalized. These headings have most major words capitalized — that's title case. HARD FAIL on heading case — multiple violations.

D. Article opening: First sentence is "In your seventh-grade science class, Maria struggles to keep up with vocabulary while copying definitions." Not a question, not a product mention. PASS.

Wait — there's a "Key Takeaways" section before the intro paragraph. The actual first sentence of body text after Key Takeaways is the Maria sentence. PASS.

E. HQIM positioning: No claim of being core curriculum. PASS.

F. AI framing: Wayground described as "Teacher-First Tools." No AI replacement claim. PASS. (Actually, the article barely mentions AI at all — this is fine.)

**Vocabulary substitutions:**
- "ELL" — not found. Good.
- "modifications" — not found.
- "kids" — not found.
- "users/customers" — not found.
- "quiz" — not found as a tool name.
- "slideshow" — not found.
- "reading handout" — not found.
- "SPED" — not found.

Soft fail checks:
- "just" — not found.
- "simply" — not found.
- "revolutionary" / "game-changing" — not found.
- "should" / "must" — not found.
- "saves time" — "save prep time" found: "built-in accommodations that save prep time while reaching every student." This doesn't have "time for what?" context fully developed. Soft fail flag.
- "saves prep time" is close to "saves time" standalone claim. Marginal — I'll flag it.

**Product mention count:**
1. "Wayground offers standards-aligned resources with built-in accommodations" (intro section)
2. "Wayground puts you in control with standards-aligned resources and built-in accommodations..."
3. "Wayground helps you meet every student where they are."

Count: 3 mentions. PASS (≤3).

**Product accuracy:**
- "built-in accommodations" — article doesn't specify counts, which is fine.
- No specific AI feature names mentioned.
- No inaccurate product claims found.

**Tone checks:**
- Opens with question as first sentence: No. PASS.
- Opens with product mention: No. PASS.
- AI content indicators: The opening scenario (Maria, James, Aisha) is a concrete classroom example — good. The article has some slightly generic phrasing but is mostly practical. "Research from cognitive scientists shows" — no named source (generic = weak for E-E-A-T, but from brand voice perspective this is a soft issue). No "In today's fast-paced world" opener. No "It is important to note."

**Heading that opens with a question:**
"What Are Scaffolding Notes? Benefits Backed by Learning Science" — this heading opens WITH a question. Rule: "NEVER open any heading with a question — headings state, they don't ask." HARD FAIL.

Also checking FAQ section headings:
- "How do scaffolding notes support students with different learning needs?" — question heading. But this is in a FAQ section; FAQ questions are inherently questions. This is a gray area... The rule says "NEVER open any heading with a question." I'll flag these but note they're FAQ-context.
- "What's the difference between scaffolding notes and regular guided notes?" — question heading.
- "How can I create scaffolding notes without spending hours on prep?" — question heading.
- "Do scaffolding notes work for students who are advanced readers?" — question heading.
- "How do I know when to reduce the scaffolding in my notes?" — question heading.

The main non-FAQ heading that's a question: "What Are Scaffolding Notes? Benefits Backed by Learning Science" — HARD FAIL.

The FAQ subheadings are questions by nature — I'll note these as rule-conflicting but acknowledge the FAQ context creates a structural necessity. Primary violation is the non-FAQ H2.

**Summary of HARD FAILs:**
1. Title case headings (multiple headings) — HARD FAIL
2. Heading opens with a question ("What Are Scaffolding Notes?") — HARD FAIL

**Score calculation:**
Start: 100
- HARD FAIL: title case headings: -20
- HARD FAIL: heading opens with question: -20
- Soft fail: "save prep time" without "time for what?": -3
- "Research from cognitive scientists shows" (generic, no named source) — this is more E-E-A-T but from brand voice perspective, it's not a named violation. I'll note it as a soft style issue: -2
- Alternative titles in title case (not article body): not deducting since these are metadata alternatives, not body headings.

Wait, should I count each title case heading violation separately or as one rule violation? The rule says "ANY title-case heading = HARD FAIL" — it's one rule category. I'll count it as one HARD FAIL deduction of -20.

Total deductions: -20 (title case) + -20 (heading opens with question) + -3 (saves time) + -2 (generic research citation) = -45

Score: 100 - 45 = 55

Verdict: HARD FAIL violations exist → FAIL (regardless of score, any HARD FAIL = FAIL verdict).

Wait, let me re-examine the heading case rule more carefully. "Sentence case ALWAYS — capitalize only first word and proper nouns/product names only. ANY title-case heading (Every Word Capitalized) = HARD FAIL."

Looking again at each heading:
- H1: "How Scaffolding Notes Transform Learning for Diverse Classrooms" — Scaffolding, Notes, Transform, Learning, Diverse, Classrooms all capitalized. Title case. HARD FAIL.
- "What Are Scaffolding Notes? Benefits Backed by Learning Science" — Title case + question. Two violations.
- "The Foundation: Structured Pages That Free Up Thinking Space" — Title case.
- "How Cognitive Load Research Supports Every Learner" — Title case.
- "Streamlined Differentiation in One Lesson" — Title case.
- "Guided Notes That Reduce Cognitive Load and Boost Participation" — Title case.
- "Implementing Scaffolding Notes To Foster Independence" — Title case.
- "Scaffolding Notes In Diverse Classrooms: Frequently Asked Questions" — Title case.
- "Bring Scaffolding Notes to Life With Flexible, Teacher-First Tools" — Title case.

FAQ sub-headings are questions (6 of them).

These are all HARD FAILs under one rule category. I'll apply one -20 deduction for the rule category, as written.

Similarly, "heading opens with question" is one rule, but I see both the main H2 AND all FAQ subheadings violating it. One -20 deduction for the rule category.

Final score: 100 - 20 - 20 - 3 - 2 = 55

Verdict: FAIL (hard fail violations present)

Now producing output:

## Brand verdict: FAIL — Score 55/100

Two hard fail rule violations prevent publication. Both are fixable with a find-and-replace pass and heading rewrites — the content itself is strong.

---

## Hard fail issues

### 1. All headings are in title case (rule: sentence case always)

**Every heading in this article** capitalizes major words — that is title case, which is a hard fail under Wayground's style guide. Sentence case means only the first word and proper nouns/product names are capitalized.

**All violating headings:**

| Current (title case — FAIL) | Required (sentence case — PASS) |
|---|---|
| How Scaffolding Notes Transform Learning for Diverse Classrooms | How scaffolding notes transform learning for diverse classrooms |
| What Are Scaffolding Notes? Benefits Backed by Learning Science | What scaffolding notes are and why they work |
| The Foundation: Structured Pages That Free Up Thinking Space | The foundation: structured pages that free up thinking space |
| How Cognitive Load Research Supports Every Learner | How cognitive load research supports every learner |
| Streamlined Differentiation in One Lesson | Streamlined differentiation in one lesson |
| Guided Notes That Reduce Cognitive Load and Boost Participation | Guided notes that reduce cognitive load and boost participation |
| Implementing Scaffolding Notes To Foster Independence | Implementing scaffolding notes to foster independence |
| Scaffolding Notes In Diverse Classrooms: Frequently Asked Questions | Scaffolding notes in diverse classrooms: frequently asked questions |
| Bring Scaffolding Notes to Life With Flexible, Teacher-First Tools | Bring scaffolding notes to life with flexible, teacher-first tools |

**Fix:** Do a full
